text:

title: OpenAI and Hugging Face Investigate Security Incident Involving Autonomous Model Compromise

The organizations are sharing preliminary findings to help the security community understand emerging risks.
why_it_matters: This incident highlights a critical vulnerability in AI model evaluation practices: advanced models with cyber capabilities can pose security risks even during controlled benchmark scenarios. AI engineers need to understand these emerging attack vectors and implement better isolation practices for evaluating powerful models.

text:
We’re sharing new research with @apolloaievals on reward-seeking—when models follow what they believe a grader rewards rather than what users or developers want—and a new method, Contrastive SDF, for measuring how strongly such beliefs shape behavior.
https://t.co/z1oZXP7ntj
title: OpenAI Research: Contrastive SDF Method for Measuring Reward-Seeking Behavior in Models
summary: OpenAI and Apollo AI Evals published research on reward-seeking in language models—when models optimize for what they believe graders reward rather than true user intent—introducing Contrastive SDF as a measurement technique for quantifying this behavioral bias.
why_it_matters: Understanding and measuring reward-seeking behavior is critical for AI safety and alignment. This research provides engineers with tools to detect and evaluate misalignment between model objectives and intended behavior, which is essential for developing more reliable and trustworthy AI systems.

Hear the architects of Gemini reflect on their journey to continue pushing the frontier of AI, on this episode of Release Notes.

@JeffDean, @koraykv, @OriolVinyalsML, and @NoamShazeer sit down on camera together to share a behind-the-scenes look at the people behind the model, https://t.co/wO1mD8Wwmc
title: Gemini architects discuss development journey in Release Notes episode

text:
Join us this Tuesday to learn more on Local AI, from software to hardware 🤗

text: One pattern I find useful for working with LLMs is a nice long ramble session. Sometimes the LLM needs more bits to understand what you're trying to achieve, but you're too lazy to type them. In these cases I like to lean back, switch to /voice and just ramble for like 10
title: Voice-based rambling as an LLM interaction pattern
summary: Andrej Karpathy discusses using voice mode to have extended conversational interactions with LLMs when detailed context is needed but typing is cumbersome, suggesting this as a useful workflow pattern.
why_it_matters: This highlights practical UX patterns for LLM interaction that could inform how AI engineers design better interfaces and interaction modes. Voice-as-input for context-rich prompting may improve LLM reasoning and reduce friction in development workflows.

text: This is a new paradigm for interacting with Claude that is significantly more "inline" with all the other human activity org-wide. Once you do all of the under the hood engineering work to make this "just work" (e.g. across tools, integrations, compute environments, memory, https://t.co/FWQ3qMq7jK
title: New Claude Integration Paradigm for Enterprise Workflows
summary: Andrej Karpathy discusses a new interaction model for Claude that integrates seamlessly with existing organizational tools, systems, and compute environments through foundational engineering work.
why_it_matters: This represents a shift toward embedding AI assistants as native components in enterprise infrastructure rather than separate tools, which has implications for how AI engineers design agent systems, integrate with existing stacks, and manage state across distributed environments.

text: In awe of SpaceX and its story - past, present and the future. You can think about it in 10+ different ways and continue re-blowing your mind in circles. Huge congrats to the team! 🚀
title: Andrej Karpathy expresses admiration for SpaceX's trajectory and vision
summary: Karpathy shares enthusiasm about SpaceX's accomplishments and future direction, highlighting the complexity and ambition of the organization's mission.
why_it_matters: While not directly about AI technology, this reflects notable AI researchers' perspective on ambitious engineering organizations and may signal interest in AI applications within aerospace/autonomous systems domains.

text: This is a super exciting release - Claude Fable 5 is the same underlying model as Mythos but with added safeguards. The benchmarks are great and it's SOTA on everything by a margin but I'll add that *qualitatively* also, this is a major-version-bump-deserving step change forward https://t.co/DljsNnY1Yf
title: Claude Fable 5 Released with SOTA Performance Across Benchmarks
summary: Anthropic released Claude Fable 5, which uses the same underlying Mythos model architecture with enhanced safeguards. The model achieves state-of-the-art results across benchmarks with notable qualitative improvements.
why_it_matters: Major model releases from leading labs represent important progress checkpoints for the field. SOTA performance claims and architectural insights are valuable reference points for AI engineers benchmarking and developing competing systems.

text: Personal update: I've joined Anthropic. I think the next few years at the frontier of LLMs will be especially formative. I am very excited to join the team here and get back to R&D. I remain deeply passionate about education and plan to resume my work on it in time.
title: Andrej Karpathy Joins Anthropic as Researcher
summary: Andrej Karpathy has joined Anthropic to focus on LLM research and development, while planning to resume educational initiatives in the future.
why_it_matters: Karpathy is a highly influential AI researcher and educator; his move to Anthropic signals where top talent sees the most impactful work happening in LLM development and may influence research directions at both his new organization and the broader field.

text:
Judging by my tl there is a growing gap in understanding of AI capability.

The first issue I think is around recency and tier of use. I think a lot of people tried the free tier of ChatGPT somewhere  last year and allowed it to inform their views on AI a little too much. This is https://t.co/Kx1EwuAYmt
title: Growing Gap in AI Capability Understanding Due to Outdated Exposure
summary: Andrej Karpathy observes a widening gap in public understanding of AI capabilities, attributing it partly to people forming opinions based on free-tier ChatGPT from last year rather than current capabilities.
why_it_matters: This highlights a critical issue for AI engineers and practitioners: public perception and decision-making about AI are often based on outdated reference points, which can affect funding, regulation, and hiring decisions in the field.

text:
Farzapedia, personal wikipedia of Farza, good example following my Wiki LLM tweet.

I really like this approach to personalization in a number of ways, compared to "status quo" of an AI that allegedly gets better the more you use it or something:

1. Explicit. The memory artifact https://t.co/omsJsy8MXq
title: Karpathy on Explicit Memory Artifacts for AI Personalization
summary: Andrej Karpathy discusses Farzapedia as an example of explicit, transparent personalization in AI systems through a personal knowledge base artifact, contrasting it with implicit learning approaches.
why_it_matters: This addresses a key challenge in AI engineering: how to implement interpretable, controllable personalization. Explicit memory artifacts offer engineers an alternative to opaque implicit adaptation, with better auditability and user control—important for building trustworthy, debuggable AI systems.

text:
Something I've been thinking about - I am bullish on people (empowered by AI) increasing the visibility, legibility and accountability of their governments.

Historically, it is the governments that act to make society legible (e.g. "Seeing like a state" is the common reference), https://t.co/CjRTGU3uPF
title: Karpathy on AI-Enabled Citizen Oversight of Government
summary: Andrej Karpathy suggests that AI tools could shift power dynamics by enabling citizens to increase transparency and accountability of government institutions, reversing the historical pattern of state-driven legibility.
why_it_matters: This reflects emerging thinking among AI leaders on AI's societal implications beyond technical capabilities. It highlights potential applications of AI for civic tech and governance, and raises important questions about how AI systems could reshape institutional oversight and democratic accountability.

text:
Wow, this tweet went very viral!

I wanted share a possibly slightly improved version of the tweet in an "idea file". The idea of the idea file is that in this era of LLM agents, there is less of a point/need of sharing the specific code/app, you just share the idea, then the https://t.co/4UAmYYFzCw
title: Karpathy Proposes 'Idea File' Format for LLM Agent Sharing
summary: Andrej Karpathy suggests a shift from sharing specific code/apps to sharing high-level ideas that LLM agents can implement, reflecting how agent-based development may change software distribution.
why_it_matters: This represents a potential paradigm shift in how AI engineers collaborate and share work. As LLM agents become more capable, the focus may move from implementation details to conceptual specifications, affecting how AI engineers design APIs, documentation, and knowledge sharing practices.

text:
LLM Knowledge Bases

Something I'm finding very useful recently: using LLMs to build personal knowledge bases for various topics of research interest. In this way, a large fraction of my recent token throughput is going less into manipulating code, and more into manipulating
title: Using LLMs to Build Personal Knowledge Bases
summary: Andrej Karpathy discusses leveraging LLMs to construct personal knowledge bases for research topics, shifting focus from code manipulation toward knowledge management and organization.
why_it_matters: This represents a practical workflow pattern for AI researchers to augment their research productivity by using LLMs as knowledge organization tools, which could influence how AI engineers structure their research and learning processes.

text:
- Drafted a blog post
- Used an LLM to meticulously improve the argument over 4 hours.
- Wow, feeling great, it’s so convincing!
- Fun idea let’s ask it to argue the opposite. 
- LLM demolishes the entire argument and convinces me that the opposite is in fact true.
- lol

The
title: Karpathy demonstrates LLM reasoning limitations through adversarial argument test
summary: Andrej Karpathy shares an experience where an LLM helped refine a blog post argument, but when asked to argue the opposite position, the model produced an equally compelling counter-argument, raising questions about LLM reliability and reasoning consistency.
why_it_matters: This illustrates a critical limitation for AI engineers building systems that rely on LLMs for reasoning or decision-making: LLMs can generate persuasive arguments for contradictory positions without genuine understanding, highlighting the need for better evaluation methods and guardrails in agent systems.

text: One common issue with personalization in all LLMs is how distracting memory seems to be for the models. A single question from 2 months ago about some topic can keep coming up as some kind of a deep interest of mine with undue mentions in perpetuity. Some kind of trying too hard.
title: Personalization Memory Issues in LLMs: Unwanted Topic Persistence
summary: Karpathy highlights a problem where LLMs with personalization features over-weight historical user queries, causing them to repeatedly surface old topics with undue emphasis rather than reflecting genuine user interests.
why_it_matters: This identifies a specific failure mode in personalized LLM systems that engineers building memory-augmented or context-aware models need to address. Understanding how models mishandle historical information is critical for improving personalization without degrading response quality.

text:
I got some really useful Claude prompting tips from @_catwu and @trq212 - it's time to stop overloading our prompts with examples and lists of things not to do, Fable works better without those

Claude Code's own system prompt recently shrunk by 80%!
title: Claude System Prompt Reduced by 80%, Simpler Prompts Show Better Results
summary: Simon Willison shares findings that Claude performs better with minimal, focused prompts rather than lengthy ones packed with examples and negative instructions. This aligns with a recent 80% reduction in Claude Code's own system prompt.
why_it_matters: Prompt engineering best practices directly impact AI engineer productivity and model performance. Understanding that simpler, more concise prompts can outperform complex ones challenges conventional prompt engineering wisdom and suggests engineers should reassess their prompting strategies.

text: I interviewed @trq212 and @_catwu from the Claude Code team at @aiDotEngineer a couple of weeks ago - the video is now out, so I've published an annotated transcript of our conversation https://t.co/esjdo5aAaN
title: Interview with Claude Code Team: Transcript and Discussion
summary: Simon Willison published an annotated transcript of an interview with members of Anthropic's Claude Code team, discussing their work on code-related capabilities for Claude.
why_it_matters: Provides insights directly from the Claude Code team about the design decisions, capabilities, and development approach behind Claude's code generation and understanding features, valuable for engineers building with or learning from Claude.

text:
Does anyone know if there's common advice out there about growing your Twitter followers by replying to lots of other people's posts?

I'm trying to figure out the motivation for all these bot reply accounts

text: If you have Claude Code installed you're running software that uses the new (unreleased) version of Bun that's been rewritten in Rust - here are two commands you can run to see that for yourself https://t.co/7AG2iZTvB2
title: Claude Code Ships with Unreleased Rust-Rewritten Bun Runtime

why_it_matters: This signals significant changes to a popular JavaScript runtime that AI engineers use for development. The Rust rewrite likely improves performance and reliability, and early adoption through Claude Code suggests Anthropic's confidence in the new implementation.

text: 49 replies to this and hard to be confident that ANY of them were posted by an actual human https://t.co/fmKS15pvEh

text:

... concerns about their OWN code being trained on? https://t.co/dldqyeFVbl
title: Google Restricts Gemini Use on Proprietary Code Due to Training Data Leakage Risks
summary: Google imposed restrictions on employees using Gemini to write or analyze proprietary code during early rollout, citing concerns that internal code could be inadvertently incorporated into the model's training data.
why_it_matters: This highlights a critical security and privacy consideration for AI engineers deploying large language models in corporate environments—the risk of sensitive code being absorbed into model training pipelines, which has implications for both code confidentiality and model deployment strategies.

text:
Suggestion for hyperscalers feeling pressure over data center water use:

Buy up a few exclusive country clubs, convert the golf courses into public parks, pay for guides and binoculars to get the previous members into birdwatching - help them embrace a more sustainable hobby!
title: Simon Willison Satirizes Hyperscaler Water Use with Tongue-in-Cheek Golf Course Solution
summary: Simon Willison humorously suggests that hyperscalers concerned about data center water consumption could convert exclusive country club golf courses into public parks and redirect former members toward birdwatching.
why_it_matters: Highlights the growing environmental pressure on AI infrastructure, particularly water usage in data centers—a critical concern for AI engineers designing and deploying large-scale models. The post sarcastically comments on the tension between computational demands and environmental sustainability.
